Introduction

Why Chemical Looping?

Chemical Looping Combustion (CLC) is an emerging clean combustion technology that enables inherent CO₂ capture without energy-intensive separation steps.

The energy sector is responsible for approximately 40% of global CO₂ emissions. Conventional carbon capture requires post-combustion scrubbing with amines or pre-combustion gasification — both energy-intensive and costly. CLC offers a fundamentally different approach: the fuel never mixes with air. Oxygen is transferred via a solid metal oxide carrier, producing a pure CO₂/H₂O stream ready for sequestration.

Our research focuses on in-situ gasification chemical looping combustion (iG-CLC) of coal using iron-based oxygen carriers. The system comprises two interconnected fluidized bed reactors — an Air Reactor (AR) where the carrier is oxidized, and a Fuel Reactor (FR) where the carrier releases oxygen to gasify and combust the fuel.

Key advantages include: no NOₓ formation (no N₂ in fuel reactor), inherent CO₂ capture (pure CO₂ stream), lower energy penalty (~4-6% vs. 25-30% for amine scrubbing), and syngas co-production capability via chemical looping gasification (CLG) mode.

Process Flow Diagram

iG-CLC System
Complete process flow diagram showing Air Reactor, Fuel Reactor, cyclones, loop seals, heat exchangers, and gas analysis train with sub-reaction thermodynamics.
Process Mechanism

Redox Chemistry & Reactor Design

The core of CLC is the cyclic reduction and oxidation of a metal oxide oxygen carrier, typically iron oxide (Fe₂O₃), circulating between two reactors.

01

🔥 Air Reactor (Oxidation)

Reduced metal oxide (FeO/Fe₃O₄) is transported to the Air Reactor and oxidized with air at 850–950°C. The highly exothermic reaction releases heat and regenerates the oxygen carrier to Fe₂O₃.

4Fe₃O₄ + O₂ → 6Fe₂O₃  ΔH° = −1118 kJ/mol
4FeO + O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃  ΔH° = −544 kJ/mol
02

⚗️ Fuel Reactor (Reduction)

The oxidized carrier (Fe₂O₃) flows to the Fuel Reactor where it provides lattice oxygen for coal gasification and combustion. The carrier is reduced while converting fuel to CO₂ and H₂O (or syngas in CLG mode).

3Fe₂O₃ + CO → 2Fe₃O₄ + CO₂  ΔH° = −24 kJ/mol
Fe₂O₃ + CO → 2FeO + CO₂  ΔH° = −11 kJ/mol
3Fe₂O₃ + H₂ → 2Fe₃O₄ + H₂O  ΔH° = −33 kJ/mol
03

🔄 Gasification (CLG Mode)

In Chemical Looping Gasification mode, partial oxidation produces syngas (H₂ + CO) rather than complete combustion products. Steam or CO₂ serves as the fluidizing/gasifying agent.

C + H₂O → CO + H₂  ΔH° = +131 kJ/mol
C + CO₂ → 2CO  ΔH° = +172 kJ/mol
CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂  ΔH° = −41 kJ/mol
04

♻️ Solid Circulation

Loop seals with N₂/Ar fluidization prevent gas mixing between reactors while allowing continuous solids circulation. Cyclones separate solids from gas streams at each reactor exit.

Solids circulation rate: 5–15 kg/m²·s
Carrier residence time: 5–20 min/cycle
Target inventory: 200–500 kg/MW
05

📊 Heat Integration

Heat exchangers recover energy from hot depleted air and syngas streams. The exothermic oxidation in the AR provides the heat needed for endothermic gasification in the FR, enabling autothermal operation.

AR outlet: 950–1000°C (depleted air)
FR outlet: 850–920°C (syngas/CO₂)
Heat recovery: >85% efficiency
06

🎯 CO₂ Capture

The fuel reactor produces a stream of CO₂ and H₂O only — no nitrogen dilution. Simple condensation yields >95% pure CO₂ ready for compression and storage, eliminating costly separation equipment.

CO₂ purity: 95–99 vol.%
Capture efficiency: >90%
Energy penalty: ~4–6% (vs. 25–30% post-combustion)
System Design

Pilot Plant Schematic & Instrumentation

Our experimental setup features a 25 kWₜₕ interconnected fluidized bed system with comprehensive temperature monitoring via MAX6675 K-type thermocouples.

CLC Pilot Plant Schematic

25 kWₜₕ Unit
Schematic of the 25 kWₜₕ CLC pilot plant showing: (1) Air Reactor, (2) Cyclones, (3) Loop Seals, (4) Heat Exchangers, (5) Fuel Reactor, (6) External Heater. Insets show oxidation of reduced metal oxide (left) and in-situ gasification mechanisms (right). Color key: orange = oxidized metal oxide, red = reduced metal oxide.

Temperature Monitoring Network

Six-channel MAX6675 thermocouple array with Arduino Mega data acquisition — real-time monitoring at 0.5 s intervals

Experimental Results

Syngas Composition Analysis

Comparative analysis of simulation vs. experimental data across varying temperatures and fuel feed rates for FR-1 and FR-2 reactor configurations.

865°C — Low Temperature Operation

Flow rate = 478.5 g/h · Both FR configurations

H₂ dominant (~50%) CO₂ ~22–28% CO ~20–25%

890°C — Optimal Temperature

Flow rate = 478.5 g/h · Both FR configurations

H₂ ~48–53% CO₂ ~22–27% CO ~22–24%

915°C — High Temperature Operation

Flow rate = 478.5 g/h · Both FR configurations

H₂ ~51–55% CO₂ ~18–23% CO ~24–26%

Temperature Effect: Increasing temperature from 865°C to 915°C shifts syngas composition toward higher H₂ and CO yields while reducing CO₂ fraction. This aligns with Le Chatelier's principle — endothermic water-gas and Boudouard reactions are favored at higher temperatures. FR-1 experimental consistently shows higher H₂ than FR-2, suggesting better steam conversion in the FR-1 configuration.

255 g/h — Low Feed Rate

890°C · FR-1 vs FR-2 comparison

High CO₂ (~45–49%) H₂ ~36–41%

487.5 g/h — Medium Feed Rate

890°C · FR-1 vs FR-2 comparison

H₂ ~47–52% CO₂ ~22–27%

600 g/h — High Feed Rate

890°C · FR-1 vs FR-2 comparison

H₂ ~50–55% CO₂ ~20–25%

Flow Rate Effect: At low feed rates (255 g/h), excess oxygen carrier promotes complete combustion, yielding high CO₂. As feed rate increases, the oxygen-to-fuel ratio decreases, shifting toward partial oxidation (gasification) with higher H₂ and CO. At 600 g/h, H₂ reaches ~50–55% while CO₂ drops to ~20%, indicating optimal gasification conditions. Simulation generally overpredicts CO₂ and underpredicts H₂ compared to experimental data, suggesting kinetic limitations in the model.

Condition Temp (°C) Flow (g/h) Config CO (%) CO₂ (%) H₂ (%) CH₄ (%) H₂/CO
Low Flow890255FR-2 Sim 11.049.337.0—3.36
Low Flow890255FR-2 Exp 13.547.236.22.12.68
Low Flow890255FR-1 Sim 12.245.040.8—3.34
Low Flow890255FR-1 Exp 14.044.838.52.72.75
Med Flow890487.5FR-2 Sim 22.826.849.6—2.18
Med Flow890487.5FR-2 Exp 23.327.047.52.22.04
Med Flow890487.5FR-1 Sim 23.823.551.8—2.18
Med Flow890487.5FR-1 Exp 22.822.052.52.72.30
High Flow890600FR-2 Sim 24.223.250.0—2.07
High Flow890600FR-2 Exp 23.024.850.02.22.17
High Flow890600FR-1 Sim 25.020.552.0—2.08
High Flow890600FR-1 Exp 22.520.055.02.52.44
📈

Key Findings

• H₂ yield increases with both temperature and flow rate, reaching 55% at 915°C/600 g/h for FR-1 experimental.

• FR-1 outperforms FR-2 in H₂ production across all conditions, likely due to improved gas-solid contact and residence time.

• Simulation vs. experiment gap is most pronounced at low flow rates, where kinetic limitations in char gasification are not fully captured.

• CH₄ remains low (<3%) across all conditions, indicating effective methane reforming by the oxygen carrier.

🎯

Optimal Conditions

For maximum H₂: 915°C, 600 g/h, FR-1 configuration → 55% H₂

For balanced syngas (H₂/CO ≈ 2): 890°C, 487.5 g/h, FR-2 → H₂/CO = 2.04

For Fischer-Tropsch feed: H₂/CO ≈ 2 is ideal; achieved at medium flow rates.

For pure H₂ production: Higher temperatures favor water-gas shift; consider adding CaO for in-situ CO₂ adsorption to push WGS forward.
